front: Poem: "Things fall apart; the centre cannot hold"; a "rough beast slouches towards Bethlehem to be born"
Poem: "vast image out of Spiritus Mundi" of a shape with "lion body and the head of a man"
Poem: repeats "A terrible beauty is born" while mourning "MacDonagh and MacBride / And Connolly and Pearse"
Poem: speaker recalls saying "polite meaningless words" to people later executed; describes a "drunken, vainglorious lout"
Poem: opens "That is no country for old men"; aged man described as "a tattered coat upon a stick"
Poem: asks sages in "God's holy fire" to "consume my heart away"; heart is "fastened to a dying animal"
Poem: speaker observes "nine-and-fifty" of the title birds that "paddle in the cold"
back: The Wild Swans at Coole (W. B. Yeats)

front: Poem: ends "How can we know the dancer from the dance?"; discusses Plato who "thought nature but a spume"
Poem: opens "I will arise and go now"; speaker plans "nine bean rows" where "peace comes dropping slow"
back: The Lake Isle of Innisfree (W. B. Yeats)

front: Poem: speaker driven by "a lonely impulse of delight"; declares "Those that I fight I do not hate"
back: An Irish Airman Foresees His Death (W. B. Yeats)
work: An Irish Airman Foresees His Death

front: Poem: "a shudder in the loins engenders there / The broken wall, the burning roof and tower / And Agamemnon dead"
back: Leda and the Swan (W. B. Yeats)

front: Poem: ends at "the foul rag and bone shop of the heart"; opens "I sought a theme and sought for it in vain"
back: The Circus Animals' Desertion (W. B. Yeats)

front: Poet: wrote "stirred my blood as nothing has for years" in his preface to Tagore's Gitanjali
Poet: mystical tract A Vision posits "double gyres" and phases of the moon, based on automatic writing with wife Georgie Hyde-Lees
Poet: adapted Noh theater in Four Plays for Dancers including At the Hawk's Well about Cuchulain
